ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for sausage bun as a filled bread product; mapped to parent class 'bread product' as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and similar products; no specific filled-bread or sausage-bun sub-code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue; mapped to category level.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No defensible SNOMED CT code identified for sausage bun as a composite food product; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard sausage bun weighs approximately 100 g and is typically consumed as a single breakfast or snack portion.

nutrient_source.title: Sausage Bun — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sausage Bun (cr_id F05020107). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated_fat_g of 3.8 g is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for sausage bread roll as the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat.

gi_source.title: White bread GI reference — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate applied to sausage bun
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for sausage bun exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 71 is estimated from white bread (GI ~71, University of Sydney GI Database), which is the closest approved-source analogue for the refined wheat-flour dough component. The pork sausage filling has negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load calculated as: GI (71) × available carbohydrate per serving (33.9 g per 100 g × 1.0 serving) / 100 = 24.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 525 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 400 mg per 100 g threshold; processed pork sausage filling contributes additional sodium from curing salts and nitrates
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit sausage bun consumption to occasional small portions and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods such as soy sauce-based dishes or processed snacks in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~71) and 33.9 g carbohydrate per 100 g with only 1.4 g dietary fibre; refined white-flour dough promotes rapid postprandial glucose elevation
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should restrict sausage bun intake to half a bun per occasion, pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to blunt glycemic response, and prefer wholemeal-dough variants where available.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Processed pork sausage is a cured red meat associated with elevated cardiovascular risk; combined saturated fat (~3.8 g per 100 g), high sodium (525 mg per 100 g), and likely trans fat from commercial shortening in the dough compound cardiovascular burden
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should treat sausage buns as an occasional food, limit portion to half a bun, and choose lower-sodium, lower-fat sausage variants to reduce saturated fat and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Processed sausage filling is high in arachidonic acid and sodium nitrates, both pro-inflammatory; commercial dough may contain hydrogenated oils contributing trans fat
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid regular consumption of sausage buns; the processed meat filling and potential trans fat from commercial shortening amplify systemic inflammatory cytokine activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A sausage bun is a popular Singaporean bakery item consisting of a soft, slightly sweet white-flour bread roll encasing a whole or sliced pork sausage. The dough is typically enriched with egg, sugar, and vegetable shortening, then baked until golden, yielding a pillowy, mildly sweet exterior that contrasts with the savoury, smoky sausage filling. It is widely sold at local bakeries, coffee shops (kopitiams), and convenience stores, and is a common breakfast or mid-morning snack. Nutritionally, each 100 g provides 284 kcal, 11.2 g protein, 11.5 g fat, and 33.9 g carbohydrate with only 1.4 g dietary fibre, reflecting a refined-carbohydrate-dominant profile. Sodium is high at 525 mg per 100 g, driven by both the bread dough and the cured sausage filling. The high glycemic index (~71) and low fibre content make this food unsuitable for frequent consumption by individuals managing type 2 diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The processed pork sausage is also pro-inflammatory, raising concern for those with rheumatoid arthritis. Calcium (69 mg) and iron (1.5 mg) are present in modest amounts from the enriched flour and meat.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a sausage bun?
Is sausage bun high in sodium?
What is the GI of sausage bun?
Is sausage bun suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020107) with confidence 0.95. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using white bread as the closest approved analogue; this introduces minor uncertainty but does not reduce overall confidence below high given the strong primary nutrient source.
notes: sugar_g is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g per the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database entry and is plausible for an unsweetened sausage filling component, though the bread dough typically contains a small amount of sugar — the HPB composite value is accepted as reported. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Sausage Bun (cr_id F05020107) does not report these values, and no other approved source (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, FAO/INFOODS SEA) provides a sufficiently matched composite entry to supply them without unacceptable estimation risk: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These fields are set to -1 (data not available) per the sentinel-value convention; the HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16 does not publish a detailed fatty acid profile, full micronutrient panel, or moisture content for this composite bakery product, and no approved secondary source provides a sufficiently close match to justify numeric estimation for these fields. Saturated_fat_g of 3.8 g is adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (sausage bread roll) as the HPB record does not separately report saturated fat; this adaptation is documented in nutrient_source. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate from white bread per University of Sydney GI Database; no direct sausage bun GI entry exists in any approved source.
